61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
150	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
151	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
152	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
153	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
154	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
155	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
156	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
157	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
158	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
159	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
160	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
161	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
162	\end{proof}
163	
164	\section{Projective dimension}
165	\label{sec:ab_pd}
166	
167	\begin{definition}
168	
169	\leanok
170	  [Projective dimension]
171	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
172	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
173	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
174	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
175	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
176	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
177	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
178	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
179	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
180	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
181	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
182	  \[
183	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
184	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
185	  \]
186	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
187	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
188	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
189	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
190	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
191	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
192	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
193	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
194	\end{definition}
195	
196	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
197	\begin{itemize}
198	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
199	    iff \(M\) is free);
200	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
201	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
202	\end{itemize}
203	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
204	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
205	
206	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
207	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
208	
209	\begin{lemma}
210	
211	\leanok
212	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
213	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
214	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
215	  
216	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
217	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
220	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
221	  %  \begin{enumerate}
222	  %  \item
223	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
228	  %  \end{enumerate}"
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
230	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
231	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
232	  \(R\)-modules. Then
233	  \begin{enumerate}
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
237	  \end{enumerate}
238	\end{lemma}
239	
240	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
241	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
242	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
243	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
244	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
245	%  from Lemma REF."
246	\begin{proof}
247	  
248	  
249	  
250	  
251	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
252	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
253	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
254	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
255	  \[
256	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
257	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
258	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
259	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
260	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
261	  \]
262	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
263	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
264	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
265	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
269	\label{sec:ab_main}
270	
271	\begin{theorem}
272	
273	\leanok
274	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
275	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
276	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
277	  
278	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
279	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
282	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
283	  %  \(\)
284	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
285	  %  \(\)"
286	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
287	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
288	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
289	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
290	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
291	  \[
292	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
293	  \]
294	\end{theorem}
295	
296	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
297	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
298	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
299	%  \(\)
300	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
301	%  \(\)
302	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
303	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
304	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
305	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
306	%  Proposition REF we see that
307	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
308	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
309	%  \begin{align*}
310	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
311	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
312	%  \ldots,\\
313	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
314	%  \end{align*}
315	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
316	%  \begin{align*}
317	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
318	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
319	%  \ldots,\\
320	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
321	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
322	%  \end{align*}
323	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
324	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
325	%
326	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
327	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
328	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
329	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
330	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
331	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
332	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
333	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
334	%  \(\)
335	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
336	%  \(\)
337	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
338	%  shows that
339	%  \(\)
340	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
341	%  M/xM \to 0
342	%  \(\)
343	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
344	%  By Lemma REF we have
345	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
346	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
347	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
348	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
349	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
350	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
351	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
352	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
353	\begin{proof}
354	  
355	  
356	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
357	
358	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
359	  finite free resolution
360	  \[
361	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
362	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
363	  \]
364	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
365	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
366	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
367	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
368	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
369	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
370	  sequences
371	  \[
372	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
373	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
374	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
375	  \]
376	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
377	  yields the chain of inequalities
378	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
379	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
380	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
381	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
382	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
383	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
384	
385	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
386	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
387	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
388	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
389	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
390	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
391	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
392	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
393	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
394	  \[
395	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
396	  \]
397	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
398	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
399	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
400	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
401	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
402	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
403	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
404	  \[
405	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
406	  \]
407	  Substituting yields
408	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
409	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
410	\end{proof}
411	
412	\begin{lemma}
413	
414	\leanok
415	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum: inductive step \(\text{pd} = k+1\)]
416	  \label{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
417	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
418	  % NOTE iter-199 review (per lean-vs-blueprint-checker ab-iter199 F-3):
419	  % the pinned declaration at AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean:1398 is `private`.
420	  % sync_leanok may not resolve private decls cleanly. Two resolution
421	  % options (the iter-200 plan agent / refactor subagent should pick):
422	  %   (1) remove `private` from the Lean declaration (preferred, since
423	  %       the blueprint has a full lemma block with proof sketch and
424	  %       the helper is logically public-facing within the AB chain);
425	  %   (2) remove this `\lean{...}` pin and keep the helper documented
426	  %       in prose only.
427	  \uses{thm:auslander_buchsbaum, lem:depth_drops_by_one}
428	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum),
429	  induction-on-\(\text{pd}\) re-organisation; cf.\ Matsumura, Theorem 19.1.}
430	
431	  Inductive-step variant pinning the project-internal helper used by
432	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}: for a Noetherian local ring
433	  \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite \(R\)-module \(M\) of projective
434	  dimension \(k+1\), and the AB formula known for projective dimension
435	  \(\leq k\), one has
436	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\).
437	\end{lemma}
438	\begin{proof}
439	  \uses{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
440	  Replay of the induction step in the main proof's
441	  \emph{inductive step} paragraph (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum},
442	  proof block), using \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one} for the
443	  depth-drop on both \(R\) and \(M\) after quotienting by an
444	  \(M\)-regular \(x \in \mathfrak m\), plus a snake-lemma carving of
445	  the minimal resolution to extract the \(R/(x)\)-minimal resolution
446	  of \(M/xM\) of the same length. The four ingredients are:
447	  \begin{enumerate}
448	    \item Minimal-resolution carving (Stacks
449	      \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}): every finite free
450	      resolution of a finite module over a Noetherian local ring can
451	      be trimmed to a minimal one of the same projective dimension.
452	    \item ``What is exact'' criterion (Stacks 00MF /
453	      \texttt{proposition-what-exact}): characterises exactness in
454	      terms of depths of \(r\)-minors. Used to extract a common
455	      non-zero-divisor \(x \in \mathfrak m\) for both \(R\) and \(M\).
456	    \item Snake lemma applied to multiplication-by-\(x\) on the
457	      minimal resolution: produces a minimal resolution of \(M/xM\)
458	      over \(R/(x)\) of the same length, so
459	      \(\text{pd}_{R/(x)}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
460	    \item Depth drops by one
461	      (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}): once an \(M\)-regular element
462	      \(x \in \mathfrak m\) is fixed,
463	      \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\) and likewise on \(R\).
464	  \end{enumerate}
465	  Combining (1)-(4) with the inductive hypothesis on \(M/xM\) over
466	  \(R/(x)\) closes the inductive step. Pieces (1)-(3) remain Mathlib
467	  gaps as of \texttt{b80f227}; piece (4) is now formalised
468	  axiom-clean (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}).
469	\end{proof}
470	
471	\begin{lemma}
472	
473	\leanok
474	  [Depth drops by one under a regular element]
475	  \label{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
476	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_depth_of_isSMulRegular}
477	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
478	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LL (\texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}).}
479	
480	  For a Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite
481	  \(R\)-module \(M\), and an \(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\),
482	  \[
483	    \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M/xM) + 1 \;=\; \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M).
484	  \]
485	\end{lemma}
486	\begin{proof}
487	  Reduces to the Ext characterisation
488	  (\cref{lem:depth_via_ext}) on both sides; the short exact sequence
489	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{\,\cdot x\,} M \to M/xM \to 0\) gives a
490	  long exact sequence in \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) on which
491	  multiplication by \(x\) is zero (since \(x \in \text{Ann}_R \kappa\)),
492	  breaking the LES into short exact pieces
493	  \(0 \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M) \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M/xM) \to
494	  \text{Ext}^{i+1}(\kappa, M) \to 0\). The translation to the
495	  \(\mathbb N_\infty\) depth equality goes through
496	  \texttt{ENat.forall\_natCast\_le\_iff\_le}.
497	\end{proof}

499	\subsection{Inductive-step substrate: per-gap decomposition of
500	  \texorpdfstring{\(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\)}{pd(M) = k+1}}
501	\label{subsec:succ_pd_gap_sequence}
502	
503	The inductive step
504	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
505	of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula is gated, as of the current pinned
506	Mathlib snapshot, by exactly three absent substrate ingredients
507	(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth that is now in hand
508	(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
509	decomposition that the proof block of
510	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} folds into four bullets:
511	its purpose is to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort
512	estimates explicit, so that the substrate gaps can be formalised
513	independently (and where the dependency graph permits, in parallel).
514	
515	\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are
516	\emph{not} mutually independent at the substrate level. Gap (3)
517	(snake-lemma carving of the minimal resolution by an
518	\(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\)) needs gap (1)
519	(minimal-resolution normalisation: every finite free resolution of
520	a finite module over a Noetherian local ring trims to a minimal one
521	of the same projective dimension) as input, because the snake-lemma
522	output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)
523	over \(R/(x)\) of the same length when the input resolution is itself
524	minimal. Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the ``what is exact'' criterion in
525	terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
526	independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
527	self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
528	feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).
529	
530	\paragraph{Dependency diagram.} Read top-to-bottom as ``provides
531	input to'':
532	\begin{verbatim}
533	                gap (1)
534	       minimal-resolution carving
535	   (Stacks lemma-add-trivial-complex)
536	                  |
537	                  v
538	                gap (3)
539	        snake lemma on minimal
540	        resolution under x in m
541	                  |
542	                  v
543	   +--------------+--------------+
544	   |   L1299 closure assembly    |   <--   gap (2)
545	   |   (succ-pd inductive step)  |        Stacks 00MF
546	   +-----------------------------+        "what is exact"
547	                  ^
548	                  |
549	                gap (4)
550	       depth drops by one (CLOSED;
551	       see lem:depth_drops_by_one)
552	\end{verbatim}
553	Gap (4) is already formalised
554	(\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}, the project's
555	\texttt{depth\_quotSMulTop\_succ\_eq\_depth\_of\_isSMulRegular})
556	and feeds the closure assembly directly. Gap (2) feeds the closure
557	assembly independently of the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3) chain.
558	
559	\paragraph{Per-gap effort estimates.} The following table records,
560	for each gap, the estimated formalisation effort (lines of code in
561	the project's Lean source style), a coarse iter budget, the
562	Mathlib-at-\texttt{b80f227} status, and substrate dependencies on
563	other gaps:

566	    \hline
567	    Gap & Statement                              & LOC est.  & Iters
568	        & Mathlib status     & Depends on \\

571	        & per-syzygy step CLOSED iter-199
572	                                                                & none \\

574	        & absent             & none \\

582	The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,
583	\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the
584	minimal-resolution induction (\S1.5): the trimming algorithm is a
585	finite-rank linear-algebra computation followed by induction on the
586	length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
587	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
588	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
589	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
590	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
591	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
592	the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream
593	Mathlib contribution rather than a project-internal proof. The gap
594	(3) estimate covers the snake-lemma application that converts a
595	minimal resolution of \(M\) into a minimal resolution of \(M/xM\)
596	over \(R/(x)\) of the same length, conditional on gap (1).
597	
598	\paragraph{Closure assembly.} Once gaps (1) and (3) and gap (2) are
599	all formalised axiom-clean, the body of
600	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
601	(the \(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\) inductive step in
602	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}) closes
603	in an estimated 80--120 LOC of assembly. The assembly proceeds:
604	(a) invoke gap (2) on the start of a minimal finite free resolution
605	of \(M\) to extract a common \(M\)- and \(R\)-regular element
606	\(x \in \mathfrak m\); (b) invoke gap (1) to normalise the
607	minimal-resolution structure; (c) invoke gap (3) on the snake-lemma
608	diagram, obtaining a minimal resolution of \(M/xM\) over \(R/(x)\)
609	of length \(\text{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), so
610	\(\text{pd}_{R/(x)}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\);
611	(d) apply gap (4)
612	(\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}) twice, once on \(R\) and once on
613	\(M\), to deduce
614	\(\text{depth}(R/(x)) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
615	\(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\); (e) close by the
616	inductive hypothesis on \((R/(x), M/xM)\), since
617	\(\text{pd}_{R/(x)}(M/xM) = k+1\) but the induction is on
618	\(\text{depth}(M)\), which has strictly decreased. The arithmetic
619	\(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) - 1 = \text{depth}(R) - 1\)
620	rearranges to the stated formula.
621	
622	\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
623	estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
624	at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly
625	at 1 iter gives a total remaining iter budget of \textbf{5--8 iters}
626	for \cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}. The schedule may
627	parallelise across the gap (2) branch and the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3)
628	chain --- so wall-clock could be as low as
629	\(\max(\text{gap (2)},\, \text{gap (1)} + \text{gap (3)}) +
630	\text{closure}\), i.e.\ \(\max(2\text{--}3,\, 2\text{--}4) + 1\),
631	yielding 3--5 iters in the parallel-best case. The substrate
632	sequencing constraint (gap (3) requires gap (1)) is hard; the gap
633	(2) branch is genuinely independent of that chain and could be
634	dispatched as a parallel prover lane the moment a free slot opens.
635	This estimate refines the earlier ``~6--12 iters'' coarse budget

641	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
642	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
643	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
644	
645	\begin{lemma}
646	
647	\leanok
648	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
649	    over a local ring]
650	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
651	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
652	  \uses{def:depth}
653	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
654	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
655	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
656	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
657	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
658	  %     \ldots
659	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
660	  %     R^{n_i}
661	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
662	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
663	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
664	  %     \ldots
665	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
666	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
667	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
668	  %  complex
669	  %     \ldots \to
670	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
671	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
672	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
673	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
674	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
675	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
676	  %     \ldots
677	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
678	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
679	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
680	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
681	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
682	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
683	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
684	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
685	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
686	  \begin{enumerate}
687	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
688	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
689	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
690	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
691	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
692	      summands).
693	  \end{enumerate}
694	\end{lemma}
695	\begin{proof}
696	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
697	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
698	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
699	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
700	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
701	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
702	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
703	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
704	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
705	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
706	
707	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
708	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
709	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
710	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
711	
712	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
713	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
714	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
715	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
716	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
717	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
718	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
719	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
720	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
721	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
722	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
723	\end{proof}
724	
725	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
726	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
727	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
728	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). The full minimal-free-resolution
729	construction (gap (1) proper) iterates this step on
730	\(\ker f \cong M_1\) (the first syzygy), then on
731	\(\ker f_1 \cong M_2\), \dots. Under the additional assumption
732	\([\textsf{IsNoetherianRing}\,R]\) each syzygy is again finite, so
733	the iteration produces a chain complex
734	\(\dots \to R^{n_2} \to R^{n_1} \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0\) with all
735	transition maps having image in \(\mathfrak m\) (the minimality
736	condition). The iter-200 follow-on lane is the
737	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive assembly into a
738	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\,(\texttt{ModuleCat}\,R)\) of
739	finite-rank free modules (gap (1)'s final form).

741	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
742	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
743	
744	\begin{definition}
745	
746	\leanok
747	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
748	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
749	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
750	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
751	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
752	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
753	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
754	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
755	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
756	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
757	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
758	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
759	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
760	\end{definition}
761	
762	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
763	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
764	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
765	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
766	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
767	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
768	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
769	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
770	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
771	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
772	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
773	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
774	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
775	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
776	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
777	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
778	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
779	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
780	\begin{corollary}
781	
782	\leanok
783	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
784	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
785	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
786	  
787	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
788	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
789	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
790	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
791	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
792	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
793	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
794	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
795	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
796	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
797	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
798	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
799	\end{corollary}
800	
801	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
802	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
803	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
804	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
805	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
806	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
807	\begin{proof}
808	  
809	  
810	  
811	  
812	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
813	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
814	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
815	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
816	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
817	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
818	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
819	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
820	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
821	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
822	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
823	
824	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
825	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
826	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
827	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
828	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
829	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
830	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
831	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
832	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
833	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
834	\end{proof}
835	
836	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
837	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
838	
839	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
840	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
841	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
842	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
843	
844	\begin{enumerate}
845	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
846	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
847	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
848	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
849	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
850	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
851	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
852	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
853	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
854	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
855	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
856	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
857	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
858	\end{enumerate}
859	
860	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
861	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the

1176	`R`-linear map `f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M` of the **minimal possible rank**
1177	`n = dim_κ (κ ⊗_R M)` (where `κ = R/𝔪` is the residue field) whose **kernel

1179	finite free resolution*: iterating the construction on the kernel (which is
1180	itself finitely generated when `R` is Noetherian) produces successive
1181	syzygies whose differential maps each have image in `𝔪` times their target.

1184	`lemma-add-trivial-complex`) in the Auslander–Buchsbaum closure chain

1198	lemma exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing
1199	    (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1200	    (M : Type u) [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M] :
1201	    ∃ (n : ℕ) (f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M),
1202	      Function.Surjective f ∧
1203	      n = _root_.Module.finrank (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R)
1204	        (TensorProduct R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R) M) ∧
1205	      LinearMap.ker f ≤ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • ⊤ := by
1206	  set κ := IsLocalRing.ResidueField R with hκ
1207	  set n := _root_.Module.finrank κ (TensorProduct R κ M) with hn
1208	  -- Pick a κ-basis of `κ ⊗_R M`.
1209	  let b : _root_.Module.Basis (Fin n) κ (TensorProduct R κ M) :=
1210	    _root_.Module.finBasis κ (TensorProduct R κ M)
1211	  -- The canonical map `(1 : κ) ⊗_R -` is surjective.
1212	  have hsurj_mk : Function.Surjective ((TensorProduct.mk R κ M) 1) := by
1213	    apply TensorProduct.mk_surjective
1214	    exact Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
1215	  -- Lift each basis element to a representative in M.
1216	  choose lift hlift using hsurj_mk
1217	  let m : Fin n → M := fun i => lift (b i)
1218	  have hm : ∀ i, (1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] m i = b i := fun i => hlift (b i)
1219	  -- Define `f` by sending each standard basis vector of `Fin n → R` to `m i`.
1220	  let f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M := (Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m
1221	  -- Evaluation: `f x = Σ x i • m i`.
1222	  have hf_eval : ∀ x : Fin n → R, f x = ∑ i, x i • m i := by
1223	    intro x
1224	    rw [show f x = ((Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R) m x from rfl,
1225	        _root_.Module.Basis.constr_apply]
1226	    have h : (Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).repr x = Finsupp.equivFunOnFinite.symm x := by
1227	      ext i; rw [Pi.basisFun_repr]; rfl
1228	    rw [h, Finsupp.sum_fintype _ _ (by intros; simp)]
1229	    exact Finset.sum_congr rfl (fun i _ => by simp)
1230	  -- Range = span of `m`.
1231	  have hf_range : LinearMap.range f = Submodule.span R (Set.range m) :=
1232	    _root_.Module.Basis.constr_range _ _
1233	  -- Nakayama: span of `m i` equals all of `M`.
1234	  have hspan : Submodule.span R (Set.range m) = ⊤ :=
1235	    IsLocalRing.span_eq_top_of_tmul_eq_basis m b hm
1236	  refine ⟨n, f, ?_, rfl, ?_⟩
1237	  · exact LinearMap.range_eq_top.mp (by rw [hf_range, hspan])
1238	  · -- Kernel containment in `𝔪 • ⊤`.
1239	    intro x hx
1240	    have hfx : f x = 0 := hx
1241	    rw [hf_eval] at hfx
1242	    -- Apply `(1 : κ) ⊗_R -` to `Σ x i • m i = 0`.
1243	    have h1 : (1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] (∑ i, x i • m i) = (0 : TensorProduct R κ M) := by
1244	      rw [hfx]; exact TensorProduct.tmul_zero _ _
1245	    rw [TensorProduct.tmul_sum] at h1
1246	    -- Rewrite each summand: `1 ⊗_R (x i • m i) = residue(x i) • b i`.
1247	    have hrewrite : ∀ i, (1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] (x i • m i)
1248	        = (IsLocalRing.residue R (x i) : κ) • b i := by
1249	      intro i
1250	      rw [show ((1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] (x i • m i))
1251	          = x i • ((1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] m i) from
1252	        (TensorProduct.tmul_smul (R := R) (x i) (1 : κ) (m i))]
1253	      rw [hm i]; rfl
1254	    rw [show (∑ i, (1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] (x i • m i))
1255	        = ∑ i, (IsLocalRing.residue R (x i) : κ) • b i from
1256	      Finset.sum_congr rfl (fun i _ => hrewrite i)] at h1
1257	    -- Linear independence of `b` forces each `residue (x i) = 0`.
1258	    have hlin : LinearIndependent κ b := b.linearIndependent
1259	    have hall : ∀ i, (IsLocalRing.residue R (x i) : κ) = 0 := by
1260	      have := Fintype.linearIndependent_iff.mp hlin
1261	        (fun i => IsLocalRing.residue R (x i)) h1
1262	      exact fun i => this i
1263	    -- Convert each component-in-𝔪 to `x ∈ 𝔪 • ⊤` via `Pi.single` decomposition.
1264	    have hx_pi : ∀ i, x i ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by
1265	      intro i
1266	      have : IsLocalRing.residue R (x i) = 0 := hall i
1267	      rwa [IsLocalRing.residue_eq_zero_iff] at this
1268	    rw [show x = ∑ i, Pi.single i (x i) from (Finset.univ_sum_single x).symm]
1269	    refine Submodule.sum_mem _ ?_
1270	    intro i _
1271	    have hsingle :
1272	        (Pi.single i (x i) : Fin n → R)
1273	          = (x i) • (Pi.single i (1 : R) : Fin n → R) := by
1274	      ext j; by_cases hij : i = j <;> simp [Pi.single, Function.update, hij]
1275	    rw [hsingle]
1276	    exact Submodule.smul_mem_smul (hx_pi i) trivial

1312	    {n : ℕ} (f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M) (hf : Function.Surjective f)

1351	/-! ### Helper iter-200 Lane AB-gap1-HasPdLT (axiom-clean): depth lower bound on
1352	the kernel of a surjection from a finite free module.
1353	
1354	For a surjection `f : R^n →ₗ M` from a finite free module of rank `n ≥ 1` over a
1355	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, with both `M` and `ker f` nontrivial, the depth
1356	of `ker f` admits the lower bound
1357	```
1358	  min(depth_𝔪 R, depth_𝔪 M + 1) ≤ depth_𝔪 (ker f).
1359	```
1360	This is Stacks 00LE (3) (`depth_of_short_exact` part 3) applied to the
1361	SES `0 → ker f → R^n → M → 0`, combined with `depth_pi_const_eq_depth_of_nonempty`
1362	to identify `depth(R^n)` with `depth(R)`.
1363	
1364	This is the ≥ direction of the LES analysis on the SES; the matching ≤ direction
1365	(`depth(ker f) ≤ min(depth R, depth M + 1)`) is the residual gap needed for the
1366	inductive step of the Auslander–Buchsbaum formula. It requires either the "what
1367	is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) or a refined LES argument when
1368	`depth M < depth R`; the current substrate provides only the easy direction. -/
1369	lemma depth_ker_ge_min_of_surjection_finite_localRing
1370	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1371	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M] [Nontrivial M]
1372	    {n : ℕ} (hn : 1 ≤ n) (f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M) (hf : Function.Surjective f)
1373	    [Nontrivial (LinearMap.ker f)] :
1374	    min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R)
1375	        (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M + 1)
1376	      ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) (LinearMap.ker f) := by
1377	  -- Set up instances on the auxiliary modules.
1378	  haveI : Inhabited (Fin n) := ⟨⟨0, hn⟩⟩
1379	  haveI : Nonempty (Fin n) := ⟨default⟩
1380	  haveI : Nontrivial (Fin n → R) := Pi.nontrivial
1381	  haveI : _root_.Module.Finite R (LinearMap.ker f) := Module.IsNoetherian.finite R _
1382	  -- The SES: 0 → ker f → R^n → M → 0.
1383	  have hex : Function.Exact (LinearMap.ker f).subtype f :=
1384	    LinearMap.exact_subtype_ker_map f
1385	  have hinj : Function.Injective (LinearMap.ker f).subtype :=
1386	    Subtype.val_injective
1387	  -- Apply `depth_of_short_exact` part (3).
1388	  have htriple := depth_of_short_exact (LinearMap.ker f).subtype f hinj hf hex
1389	  have h3 := htriple.2.2
1390	  -- Rewrite `depth (Fin n → R) = depth R`.
1391	  have heq : depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) (Fin n → R)
1392	      = depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R :=
1393	    depth_pi_const_eq_depth_of_nonempty _
1394	  rw [heq] at h3
1395	  exact h3
1396	
1397	end Module

1399	/-! ## §5. The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula
1400	
1401	For a nonzero finite module `M` of finite projective dimension over a
1402	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, the **Auslander–Buchsbaum formula** reads
1403	```
1404	  pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)
1405	```
1406	(Stacks tag 090V). The proof inducts on `depth(M)`: the base case
1407	`depth(M) = 0` uses a minimal finite free resolution of `M` and the
1408	"what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) plus iterated application of the
1409	depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma (§4) to bound `depth(R)` against the
1410	resolution length; the inductive step picks a common non-zero-divisor
1411	`x ∈ 𝔪` on both `R` and `M`, applies the snake lemma to obtain a minimal
1412	finite free resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the same length, and uses
1413	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`.
1414	
1415	Blueprint reference: `thm:auslander_buchsbaum` (Stacks tag 090V). -/
1416	
1417	/-! ### Lane G iter-195 structural carving: inductive-step helper.
1418	
1419	The `pd_R(M) = k + 1` inductive step of the Auslander–Buchsbaum formula is
1420	extracted into the helper `auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd` below. This
1421	isolates the entire substrate-gap content of the inductive step into a
1422	single named typed-`sorry` declaration with a precise iter-196+
1423	re-engagement plan, rather than an opaque inline `sorry` at the case-split
1424	site. The main theorem then dispatches the `n > 0` branch by a one-line
1425	`exact` call to the helper.
1426	
1427	Per the iter-195 Lane G directive (OFF-CRITICAL-PATH minimal dispatch),
1428	this is a **structural commit** (option (b): carving with iter-196 re-
1429	engagement timeline). The HARD BAR is met; closure is deferred. -/
1430	
1431	/-- **Lane G iter-195 structural carving (typed-`sorry` substrate-gap helper).**
1432	
1433	The `pd_R(M) = k + 1` inductive step of the Auslander–Buchsbaum formula.
1434	
1435	**Blueprint proof shape** (`sec:ab_main` inductive step, Stacks 090V).
1436	Strong induction on `depth(M)`:
1437	
1438	* **Sub-case `depth(M) = 0`.** Let `e := pd_R(M) = k + 1`. By Stacks
1439	  `lemma-add-trivial-complex` (minimal-finite-free-resolution carving),
1440	  `M` admits a minimal resolution
1441	  `0 → R^{n_e} → R^{n_{e-1}} → ⋯ → R^{n_0} → M → 0` with all matrix
1442	  coefficients in `𝔪`. The "what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF)
1443	  gives `depth(R) ≥ e`. Iterating `depth_of_short_exact` on the
1444	  short-exact splits `0 → R^{n_e} → R^{n_{e-1}} → K_{e-2} → 0`, …,
1445	  `0 → K_0 → R^{n_0} → M → 0` gives
1446	  `depth(K_{e-c}) ≥ depth(R) - (c - 1)` and finally
1447	  `depth(M) ≥ depth(R) - e`. Since `depth(M) = 0`, also `depth(R) ≤ e`,
1448	  hence `depth(R) = e = (k + 1) + depth(M)`.
1449	
1450	* **Sub-case `depth(M) > 0`.** Pick `x ∈ 𝔪` a common NZD on both `R`
1451	  and `M` (Stacks `lemma-depth-sum-direct-sum` ⟹ `depth(R ⊕ M) > 0`,
1452	  hence a common NZD via prime avoidance over `Ass(R) ∪ Ass(M)`). The
1453	  snake lemma applied to multiplication by `x` on a minimal resolution
1454	  of `M` produces a minimal resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the
1455	  same length, so `pd_{R/xR}(M/xM) = k + 1`. By Stacks
1456	  `lemma-depth-drops-by-one`, `depth(R/xR) = depth(R) - 1` and
1457	  `depth(M/xM) = depth(M) - 1`, with regular sequences in `𝔪`
1458	  corresponding to those in `𝔪/(x)`, so
1459	  `depth_R(M/xM) = depth_{R/xR}(M/xM)`. The IH on `M/xM` over `R/xR`
1460	  gives `(k + 1) + (depth(M) - 1) = depth(R) - 1`, i.e.
1461	  `(k + 1) + depth(M) = depth(R)`.

1660	/-! ## §6. Cohen–Macaulay local rings
1661	
1662	A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth equals
1663	its Krull dimension (Stacks tag 00N4). Mathlib `b80f227` has neither the
1664	predicate nor the class — this file is the upstream gap-fill.
1665	
1666	Blueprint reference: `def:cohen_macaulay_local` (Stacks tag 00N4). -/
1667	
1668	/-- A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth
1669	equals its Krull dimension: `depth(R) = dim R`.
1670	
1671	Encoded as a `Prop`-valued type class so downstream consumers can write
1672	`[CohenMacaulay R]` and use Cohen–Macaulay as a hypothesis. Mathlib at the
1673	pinned commit (`b80f227`) does not expose any Cohen–Macaulay predicate;
1674	this is the upstream gap-fill.
1675	
1676	iter-176+: the predicate is `Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R =
1677	ringKrullDim R`. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the carrier definition is a
1678	typed `sorry` at the `Prop` level — substantively, the predicate is the
1679	named equality, but we package it as a `class` so use sites are uniform. -/
1680	class CohenMacaulay (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1681	    [IsNoetherianRing R] : Prop where
1682	  /-- The Cohen–Macaulay equation: `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`. The numeric
1683	  comparison is in `WithBot ℕ∞` after coercion of the `ℕ∞`-valued depth. -/
1684	  depth_eq_krullDim :
1685	    (Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R : WithBot ℕ∞) = ringKrullDim R
1686	
1687	/-! ## §7. Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay
1688	
1689	The consumer-facing input for A.4.a: every regular Noetherian local ring is
1690	Cohen–Macaulay (Stacks tag 00OD). The direct proof: pick a minimal
1691	generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of `𝔪` (where `d = dim R`), use that `R` is a
1692	domain (Stacks 00NQ) to start an `R`-regular sequence, and induct on
1693	dimension — each `R/(x_1, …, x_c)` is again regular of dimension `d - c`,
1694	so `x_1, …, x_d` is an `R`-regular sequence and `depth(R) ≥ d`. The reverse
1695	inequality `depth(R) ≤ dim R` is the standard depth bound (Stacks 00LK).
1696	
1697	Blueprint reference: `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` (Stacks tag 00OD). -/
1698	
1699	namespace CohenMacaulay
1700	
1701	/-! ### Helper 1 (axiom-clean): length-bound on regular sequences
1702	
1703	For a Noetherian local ring `R`, every `R`-regular sequence has length at most
1704	`ringKrullDim R`. This is the **upper bound** half of Stacks 00OD: it is the
1705	specialisation of the equality
1706	`ringKrullDim (R / ofList rs) + rs.length = ringKrullDim R`
1707	(`ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular`) to the observation that
1708	`ringKrullDim (R / ofList rs) ≥ 0` whenever the quotient is nontrivial, which it
1709	is precisely because `IsRegular` rules out `rs • ⊤ = ⊤`.
1710	
1711	iter-181 Lane G: closed kernel-clean. -/
1712	private lemma length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular
1713	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1714	    {rs : List R} (h : RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs) :
1715	    (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) ≤ ringKrullDim R := by
1716	  have heq := ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular rs h
1717	  have hntq : Nontrivial (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) := by
1718	    rw [Ideal.Quotient.nontrivial_iff]
1719	    intro habs
1720	    apply h.top_ne_smul
1721	    change (⊤ : Submodule R R) = (Ideal.ofList rs) • ⊤
1722	    rw [habs]; simp
1723	  have hnn : (0 : WithBot ℕ∞) ≤ ringKrullDim (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) :=
1724	    ringKrullDim_nonneg_of_nontrivial
1725	  calc (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞)
1726	      = 0 + (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) := by simp
1727	    _ ≤ ringKrullDim (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) + (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) := by gcongr
1728	    _ = ringKrullDim R := heq

1739	    (x : R) (hx : x ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)

1747	/-! ### Helper 2.0 (iter-187 Lane G sub-lane G1): cotangent dim-drop on `R ⧸ (x)`
1748	
1749	**Statement.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` and `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²`, the
1750	cotangent space of `R / (x)` has dimension one less than that of `R`:
1751	```
1752	finrank κ' (CotangentSpace (R/(x))) + 1 = finrank κ (CotangentSpace R)
1753	```
1754	where `κ = R / 𝔪` and `κ' = (R/(x)) / 𝔪'` are the two residue fields
1755	(canonically isomorphic via the natural quotient `R/𝔪 ≃ (R/(x))/𝔪'`).
1756	
1757	**Role.** This is the cotangent-space dim-drop building block for
1758	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` below: once Stacks 00NQ
1759	(regular local ⟹ domain) is filled in, this dim-drop is what upgrades
1760	`R/(x)` of dimension `k` back to `IsRegularLocalRing` via
1761	`IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace`.
1762	
1763	**Proof structure** (Bourbaki-style via `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank`):
1764	1. The R-algebra map `π : R → R/(x)` induces a κ-linear surjection of
1765	   cotangent spaces `f : 𝔪.Cotangent → 𝔪'.Cotangent` via
1766	   `Ideal.mapCotangent_surjective_of_comap_eq` (which applies because
1767	   `(maximalIdeal R).comap π = ker π ⊔ maximalIdeal R = (x) ⊔ 𝔪 = 𝔪`).
1768	2. The kernel of `f` is the R-submodule generated by the image `x̄` of `x`
1769	   in `𝔪.Cotangent` (via `Ideal.mapCotangent_ker_of_surjective` — the
1770	   kernel is `(Submodule.comap 𝔪.subtype ((x) ⊓ 𝔪)).map 𝔪.toCotangent`
1771	   which collapses to `Submodule.span R {x̄}` because `(x) ⊆ 𝔪`).
1772	3. The R-action on 𝔪.Cotangent factors through κ (it's a κ-vector space),
1773	   so this is also `Submodule.span κ {x̄}`.
1774	4. `x ∉ 𝔪²` ⟺ `x̄ ≠ 0` (Helper 1.5), giving
1775	   `finrank κ (Submodule.span κ {x̄}) = 1` via `finrank_span_singleton`.
1776	5. Combining via `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` and the
1777	   `LinearMap.quotKerEquivOfSurjective` isomorphism, we get the formula.
1778	   The residue-field switch `κ → κ'` is handled by
1779	   `IsLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_finrank_cotangentSpace`, which
1780	   reduces the κ-finrank to spanFinrank (a ring-theoretic invariant) and
1781	   the spanFinrank-equality is purely numeric.
1782	
1783	**iter-187 deliverable**: structural scaffold with the assembled body left
1784	as a single named typed sorry (the κ-subspace identification step). The
1785	preparatory lemma `toCotangent_ne_zero_of_not_mem_sq` lands axiom-clean.
1786	
1787	**iter-188+** body fill-in: instantiate steps (1)-(5) above. Key Mathlib API:
1788	* `Ideal.mapCotangent`, `Ideal.mapCotangent_surjective_of_comap_eq`,
1789	  `Ideal.mapCotangent_ker_of_surjective` (Mathlib b80f227 ✓)
1790	* `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` (Mathlib b80f227 ✓)
1791	* `finrank_span_singleton` (Mathlib b80f227 ✓)
1792	* `IsLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_finrank_cotangentSpace`
1793	  (Mathlib b80f227 ✓) — bridges κ-finrank and κ'-finrank via spanFinrank.

2041	/-! ### Helper 2 (substantive typed `sorry`): system-of-parameters as a regular sequence
2042	
2043	For a regular local ring `R` of Krull dimension `d = (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank`,
2044	a minimal generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of the maximal ideal `𝔪` is an `R`-regular
2045	sequence. This is the **lower bound** half of Stacks 00OD.
2046	
2047	The proof in Stacks 00OD uses:
2048	1. A regular local ring is an integral domain (Stacks 00NQ).
2049	2. For each `c < d`, the quotient `R / (x_1, …, x_c)` is again a regular local
2050	   ring, of Krull dimension `d - c` (Krull's principal ideal theorem +
2051	   `lemma-one-equation`).
2052	
2053	Step (1) — `IsRegularLocalRing R ⟹ IsDomain R` — is **not present in Mathlib at
2054	the pinned commit** (`b80f227`); a `lean_leansearch` for "regular local ring is
2055	a domain" returns nothing relevant. Step (2) requires the regular-quotient
2056	inductive structure (Stacks 00NQ + height-one quotient regularity), which is
2057	similarly absent. Both are substantive multi-iter content.
2058	
2059	The signature here is the non-tautological existence statement: a list `rs : List R`
2060	with `rs.length = (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank`, `rs ⊆ maximalIdeal R`, and
2061	`IsRegular R rs`. Downstream, `of_regular` consumes this directly to close the
2062	`depth ≥ d` lower bound.
2063	
2064	iter-185 Lane G **STRUCTURAL SCAFFOLD**: the main lemma is structurally
2065	complete by strong induction on `n = spanFinrank R`. The inductive step is
2066	factored into `regularLocal_inductive_step` (axiom-clean: lift-and-cons
2067	assembly via Mathlib's `IsRegular.cons'`) plus the Mathlib-gap helper
2068	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` (typed `sorry`,
2069	Stacks 00NQ + 00NU consolidated). The substrate work (`IsRegularLocalRing ⟹
2070	IsDomain` + Krull's PIT for quotient-by-minimal-generator) is genuinely
2071	multi-iter; the iter-185 progress is the structural extraction of the
2072	Mathlib gap into a narrow, well-typed helper, leaving the assembly
2073	axiom-clean for the gap-fill iteration to finish in `~5 LOC` once the
2074	helper closes. -/

2076	/-! ### Narrow substrate gap (Stacks 00NQ): regular local ring is a domain
2077	
2078	The Auslander–Buchsbaum-route closure of "regular local ring is Cohen–Macaulay"
2079	ultimately reduces to **Stacks 00NQ**: every regular local Noetherian ring
2080	is an integral domain. Mathlib at the pinned commit `b80f227` does not expose
2081	this implication (`lean_leansearch` for "regular local ring is a domain"
2082	returns nothing relevant; `lean_loogle` for `IsRegularLocalRing _ → IsDomain _`
2083	returns empty).
2084	
2085	Stacks 00NQ proof sketch (induction on `dim R = spanFinrank 𝔪`):
2086	* Base `dim R = 0`: then `𝔪 = ⊥`, hence `R` is a field, hence a domain.
2087	* Step `dim R = d + 1 ≥ 1`:
2088	  - Pick `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²` (axiom-clean via Nakayama; see
2089	    `exists_notMemSq_of_spanFinrank_pos` below).
2090	  - By the cotangent dim-drop (`finrank_cotangentSpace_quot_span_singleton_succ`,
2091	    iter-188 Lane G1 closure) **plus** Krull's principal ideal theorem
2092	    (`ringKrullDim_quotient_span_singleton_succ_eq_ringKrullDim_of_mem_jacobson`
2093	    gives the `≥` half), `R / (x)` is regular local of dim `d`.
2094	  - By induction, `R / (x)` is a domain, hence `(x)` is a prime ideal of `R`.
2095	  - Let `𝔭` be a minimal prime of `R` with `𝔭 ⊆ (x)`. For `y ∈ 𝔭`, write
2096	    `y = xz`; since `𝔭` is prime and `x ∉ 𝔭` (else `x ∈ 𝔭 ⊆ (x)` doesn't
2097	    immediately give a contradiction but combined with `dim R/(x) = d` and
2098	    `x ∈ 𝔭` height-zero we get `𝔭 = 0` which is the conclusion), we get
2099	    `z ∈ 𝔭`. Hence `𝔭 ⊆ x 𝔭`, so iterating, `𝔭 ⊆ ∩ x^n 𝔭 ⊆ ∩ 𝔪^n = 0`
2100	    (Krull intersection). So `𝔭 = 0`, and `R` is a domain.
2101	
2102	Full formalization is ~300 LOC (involves Krull's PIT, prime avoidance,
2103	Krull intersection, minimal-primes-are-finite); deferred to a future iter
2104	or Mathlib upstream contribution. -/

2264	/-! ### Helper iter-191 Lane G (axiom-clean): zero-divisor witness from a minimal prime
2265	
2266	For a commutative ring `R` and a minimal prime `𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R`, every
2267	element of `𝔭` is a zero-divisor in `R`. Concretely: for any `x ∈ 𝔭`, there
2268	exists `y ∈ R, y ≠ 0` with `x * y = 0`.
2269	
2270	Proof: minimal primes are disjoint from non-zero-divisors via
2271	`Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes`, so `x ∈ 𝔭` ⟹
2272	`x ∉ nonZeroDivisors R` ⟹ `∃ y ≠ 0, x * y = 0`.
2273	
2274	This packages the Stacks-00NQ-relevant first step for the `x ∈ 𝔭` case of
2275	`isDomain_of_regularLocal`: when `(x)` is a minimal prime, `x` is a zero-divisor,
2276	so the obstruction reduces to derivation of a contradiction from the
2277	zero-divisor witness (using the regular-local structure of `R` and `R/(x)`). -/
2278	private lemma exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes
2279	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] {𝔭 : Ideal R} (h𝔭 : 𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R)
2280	    {x : R} (hx : x ∈ 𝔭) :
2281	    ∃ y : R, y ≠ 0 ∧ x * y = 0 := by
2282	  have hdisj : Disjoint (𝔭 : Set R) (nonZeroDivisors R : Set R) :=
2283	    Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes h𝔭
2284	  have hxNot : x ∉ nonZeroDivisors R := fun hxNZD =>
2285	    (Set.disjoint_left.mp hdisj) hx hxNZD
2286	  have hExistsZD : ¬ ∀ z, x * z = 0 → z = 0 := fun h => hxNot <| by
2287	    rw [mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff]
2288	    refine ⟨h, fun z hz => h z (by rw [mul_comm]; exact hz)⟩
2289	  push Not at hExistsZD
2290	  obtain ⟨y, hxy, hy⟩ := hExistsZD
2291	  exact ⟨y, hy, hxy⟩

2296	For a regular local Noetherian ring `R` of `spanFinrank 𝔪 = k + 1 ≥ 1` and
2297	`x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²`, the ideal `Ideal.span {x}` is *not* a minimal prime of `R`.

2299	case-split scaffold isolated the obstruction to this single case.

2302	`hIH` as an explicit argument (universally quantified over the ring `R'` at
2303	dimension `k`), so it can be invoked inside `isDomain_of_regularLocal`'s
2304	`succ` arm without requiring `IsDomain R` (which is the goal we are
2305	proving). Concretely `hIH` provides `IsDomain (R / (x))` since the quotient
2306	is regular local of `spanFinrank = k` (via
2307	`regularLocal_quotient_isRegularLocal_of_notMemSq`).

2310	considered, all blocked on substrate work beyond Mathlib `b80f227`:
2311	* (i) **Graded-ring approach** (the Stacks 00NQ proof). Build the associated
2312	  graded ring `gr_𝔪 R := ⨁_n 𝔪^n / 𝔪^{n+1}`; prove the surjection
2313	  `κ[X_1,…,X_{k+1}] ↠ gr_𝔪 R` (from a regular system of parameters) is an
2314	  isomorphism (via Hilbert-Samuel function or cotangent count); deduce
2315	  `gr_𝔪 R` is a domain; conclude `R` is a domain via leading-term
2316	  multiplicativity + Krull intersection. ~500–800 LOC.

2318	  local of same dimension. By the Cohen structure theorem,
2319	  `R̂ ≅ κ̂[[T_1,…,T_{k+1}]]` is a power-series ring over a complete field,

2321	  is a domain. ~400–600 LOC (Cohen structure thm is the big substrate
2322	  cost).

2324	  the zero-divisor witness `y ≠ 0, x * y = 0` (via

2542	      (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = n → IsDomain R by

2559	      ih (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) hdim_quot
2560	    -- Step 4: (x) is prime in R (R/(x) is a domain).

2565	      (I := (⊥ : Ideal R)) (J := Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) bot_le
2566	    haveI h𝔭_prime : 𝔭.IsPrime := Ideal.minimalPrimes_isPrime h𝔭_min

2748	/-- **Lane G inductive step (iter-185 structural scaffold).** Packages the
2749	inductive step of Stacks 00OD: given a regular local ring `R` of dimension
2750	`k + 1`, plus the inductive hypothesis at dimension `k` (universally

2754	The body is **axiom-clean** modulo the typed-`sorry` substrate helper
2755	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ`. Assembly path:
2756	
2757	1. Helper extracts `x ∈ 𝔪` with `IsSMulRegular R x` AND `IsRegularLocalRing
2758	   (R⧸(x))` of `spanFinrank = k`.
2759	2. IH applied on `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` produces a regular sequence
2760	   `rs'_q : List (R ⧸ (x))` of length `k` in the maximal ideal there.
2761	3. Lift `rs'_q` to `rs : List R` via `Function.surjInv` of
2762	   `Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective`; the section property gives
2763	   `rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk _) = rs'_q`.
2764	4. Members of `rs` lie in `𝔪` because the maximal ideal of `R⧸(x)` is the
2765	   image of `𝔪` (`IsLocalRing.le_maximalIdeal` applied to the comap chain).
2766	5. Cons via `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.cons'` to form the length-`(k+1)`
2767	   sequence `x :: rs`.

2770	spreading it across the lemma body; future iterations can attack Stacks 00NQ
2771	+ 00NU directly. -/
2772	private lemma regularLocal_inductive_step {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
2773	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] {k : ℕ}
2774	    (hdim : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = k + 1)
2775	    (IH : ∀ (R' : Type u) [CommRing R'] [IsLocalRing R'] [IsNoetherianRing R']
2776	            [IsRegularLocalRing R'],
2777	            (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R').spanFinrank = k →
2778	            ∃ rs : List R', rs.length = k ∧
2779	              (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R') ∧
2780	              RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R' rs) :
2781	    ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = k + 1 ∧
2782	      (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ∧
2783	      RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs := by
2784	  -- Step 1: extract `x ∈ 𝔪` regular on `R` with `R ⧸ (x)` regular local of
2785	  -- `spanFinrank = k`.  This is the consolidated Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate.
2786	  obtain ⟨x, hxMem, hxReg, hRLR, hdim_quot⟩ :=
2787	    exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ hdim
2788	  -- Step 2: apply IH on `R ⧸ (x)` — this gives a regular sequence of length
2789	  -- `k` on the quotient, valued in its maximal ideal.
2790	  obtain ⟨rs'_q, hlen_q, hmem_q, hreg_q⟩ := IH (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}) hdim_quot
2791	  -- Step 3: lift `rs'_q : List (R ⧸ (x))` to `rs' : List R` via the right
2792	  -- inverse of the (surjective) quotient ring hom.
2793	  let mkq : R →+* R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} := Ideal.Quotient.mk _
2794	  let g : R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} → R := Function.surjInv Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
2795	  have hg : ∀ y, mkq (g y) = y := Function.surjInv_eq _
2796	  let rs' : List R := rs'_q.map g
2797	  have hlen_rs' : rs'.length = k := by simp [rs', hlen_q]
2798	  have hmkmap : rs'.map mkq = rs'_q := by
2799	    change (rs'_q.map g).map mkq = rs'_q
2800	    rw [List.map_map]
2801	    conv_rhs => rw [← List.map_id rs'_q]
2802	    exact List.map_congr_left fun y _ => hg y
2803	  -- Step 4: each element of `rs'` lies in `𝔪 R` via the comap of `𝔪 (R⧸(x))`.
2804	  -- The maximal ideal of `R ⧸ (x)` comaps back to `𝔪 R` (it's the *unique*
2805	  -- maximal ideal of `R` containing `Ideal.span {x} ⊆ 𝔪`).
2806	  have hmem_rs' : ∀ r ∈ rs', r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by
2807	    intro r hr
2808	    simp only [rs', List.mem_map] at hr
2809	    obtain ⟨y, hy_mem, rfl⟩ := hr
2810	    -- The comap of `𝔪 (R⧸(x))` under the surjective `mkq` is a maximal ideal
2811	    -- of `R`; since `R` is local, it equals `𝔪 R`.  Hence `g y ∈ comap mkq 𝔪'`
2812	    -- iff `g y ∈ 𝔪 R`.
2813	    have hmax_comap : (Ideal.comap mkq
2814	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}))).IsMaximal :=
2815	      Ideal.comap_isMaximal_of_surjective _ Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
